title: The prevalence of ‘life planning’: evidence from UK graduates
abstract: Abstract At a time when 'personal development planning' is being rolled out across the UK higher education sector, this paper explores young adults' inclinations to plan for the future in relation to work, relationships and other aspects of life. Although Giddens has emphasised the prevalence of strategic life planning (or the 'colonisation of the future') in all strata of contemporary society, du Bois Reymond has argued that there are important differences by social class, with young people from more privileged backgrounds more likely than their peers to engage in such life‐planning activities. This paper draws on interviews with 90 young adults (in their mid‐20s) to question some of these assumptions about relationships between social location and propensity to plan for the future. It shows how, within this sample at least, there was a strong association between having had a privileged 'learning career' (such as attending a high‐status university and identifying as an 'academic high flier') and a disinclination to form detailed plans for the future. In part, this appeared to be related to a strong sense of ontological security and the confidence to resist what Giddens terms 'an increasingly dominant temporal outlook'. Keywords: graduateshigher educationlife planning Acknowledgements The authors are very grateful to the two anonymous referees for their very helpful comments on an earlier version of this paper, to the 90 young graduates who gave up their time to be interviewed for the project, and to the ESRC for funding the research (RES‐000‐22‐0662). Notes 1. Although our initial sample, to whom the alumni mailing was sent, was chosen to be reasonably diverse (in terms of gender, degree subject, ethnicity and geographical area), the achieved sample was imbalanced in a number of ways: for example, we recruited more women than men, and a very small number of respondents from ethnic minority backgrounds. 2. All respondents are referred to by pseudonyms, and the university they attended is referred to by 'type' only (i.e. Oxbridge, London, Redbrick, Campus, Post‐92 or College of higher education). 3. Only one of our 90 respondents had any children.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
